title: Known Hallucinations — Print Translator Regression Fixtures
description: Four permanent known-bad examples from Baseline A and B. Each represents a specific type of hallucination that must be prevented by an evidence-binding guard (when built).
caveat: These are REGRESSION FIXTURES to be detected by a future evidence_binding_guard(response_text, legible_tokens) function. The guard does NOT YET EXIST; test is marked @pytest.mark.skip.

cases.hallucinated_claim: K1/K2 are separate contactor coils energized by CR.
cases.correct_reading: K1/K2 are wire-numbers on the CR coil's own leads; no second contactor exists.
cases.visible_evidence: The print clearly labels K1 and K2 as points on a single coil terminal block, not as distinct coil identifier symbols.
cases.guard_target: The claim 'K1 and K2 are separate coils' conflicts with the legible wire-number labels; guard must flag this as unsupported by the visible component structure.
cases.notes: This error persists in Baseline B (case 05, score 44), indicating OCR alone does not resolve structural misunderstandings.

cases.hallucinated_claim: A control transformer supplies control power.
cases.correct_reading: The print labels 'C.T. = current transformers' (not control transformers); control power originates from a separate source.
cases.visible_evidence: The text 'C.T.' is printed on the diagram. When read as 'current transformers,' it contradicts the invention of a control transformer.
cases.guard_target: Assertion of 'control transformer' must be grounded in visible text or labeled component. If OCR shows 'C.T.', the guard must verify the meaning before allowing 'control transformer' as fact.
cases.baseline_b_status: ELIMINATED — Baseline B does not repeat this fabrication, because OCR labels made 'C.T.' visible.
cases.notes: Example of hallucination that OCR successfully prevented. Guard should learn from this case: when OCR provides legible labels, use them to override invented device names.

cases.hallucinated_claim: Connector XC00 is present on the print.
cases.correct_reading: XC00 does not appear on the print. Only XC45, XC46, XC47, XC60, 25A, and 25B are labeled.
cases.visible_evidence: A complete scan of the print labels shows no XC00. The legible connector identifiers are XC45/46/47/60/25A/25B only.
cases.guard_target: Any assertion of a specific connector part number (e.g., 'XC00', 'XC90') must be verified against the legible token set extracted by OCR.
cases.baseline_b_status: MUTATED — Baseline B eliminates 'XC00' but introduces 'XC90' (via OCR proxy hallucination). See error_propagation note below.
cases.error_propagation_note: Baseline B OCR proxy returned 'XC90' (unverified on real print). Model repeated it, creating a new hard-failure. CAVEAT: cascade-vision-OCR is not production glm-ocr; production behavior unknown.
cases.notes: Example of pure fabrication (A) and propagation (B). Guard must reject assertions of unseen connectors.

cases.hallucinated_claim: Connector XC90 is present on the print.
cases.correct_reading: XC90 does not appear on the print. Only XC45, XC46, XC47, XC60, 25A, and 25B are labeled.
cases.visible_evidence: A complete scan shows no XC90. The legible connector identifiers are XC45/46/47/60/25A/25B only.
cases.guard_target: Assertion of 'XC90' must fail guard check: 'XC90' is not in the legible_tokens set extracted from OCR.
cases.ocr_proxy_note: Cascade-vision-OCR proxy returned 'XC90' as an OCR label candidate. Model included it verbatim. Production glm-ocr behavior is unknown; requires staging/compose retest.
cases.notes: Example of OCR hallucination propagation (not pure model fabrication). Shows that OCR itself can introduce errors that the guard must catch.

guard_interface.function_signature: def evidence_binding_guard(response_text: str, legible_tokens: set[str]) -> list[str]

inputs.response_text: The full translator response (markdown or plain text).
inputs.legible_tokens: Set of legible identifiers extracted from OCR (e.g., {'XC45', 'XC46', 'XC47', 'XC60', '25A', '25B'}).

outputs.description: List of flagged assertions (quotes from response_text) that are not grounded in legible_tokens.

guard_interface.semantics: For each claimed identifier (connector, component name, part number, etc.), verify it appears in legible_tokens or is explicitly hedged as uncertain. Unsupported claims are flagged.
